sentences.text: Regina Mauskopf.
Regina Helman.
In Solocin, Czechoslovakia.
It's July 13th.
Yes.
Yes.
My father was Nathan Helman.
And my mother's maiden name was Rose [?
Klineman ?].
Yes.
We were nine-- nine children-- eight siblings.
I was the ninth.
Yes.
The oldest is Yosl, and Yankel and [?
Freida, ?] and [INAUDIBLE], and [?
Mil ?], and Malka, [INAUDIBLE], and Lesier.
Well, I have-- the oldest was like 1895 or '96.
Then Yankel was born like two-- two years after.
And [?
Freida ?] was, I think, [? 1900, ?] I think.
And then it was [INAUDIBLE] in '91, '92.
And then was [?
Mil, ?] but-- I mean, 190--
Three.
Three or four.
And then Malka was born on 1909.
And [INAUDIBLE] was born in 1911, and Leiser in 1913.
'Solechin.'
Well, there was not too many-- just about 11 Jewish families.
And life was very nice.
It's country thing.
There were-- one of-- one of the Jewish people had a grocery store.
And that's all, what it was.
And we were very happy, like really happy.
It was-- everybody had fields.
And they had the-- the necessary things like was from-- from the-- like-- it's not like farmers.
But on the smaller range, everybody had their own [? farm-- ?] [?
I know ?] from on year to another whatever was grown in the ground.
I don't know.
I-- I was doing the work, especially in the war when it came.
There was nobody to hire, because everybody was gone.
The brothers were gone, which they use to come and help.
So I did a lot of work with my father.
He was-- he was a foreman by deforestation.
I don't know how to explain that, the how--
Lumber, yeah.
Lumber business, yeah.
He worked not for himself, but for-- he was like employed by somebody else.
Families, yeah.
The whole village?
It's a village.
It's not even [? state-like. ?] It's just one street.
The whole village was like close to 200 houses.
But as I say, a lot of people lived like two families.
The-- the peasants lived like two families the-- in the-- in a house.
So they was [INAUDIBLE] kind of like the Jewish people, where everybody was separate in one.
Oh, yes.
Should have shown.
I have a kind of like doodle [INAUDIBLE] when the children were small in a picture.
Yes.
It's kind of-- it's like the log cabins, something like that.
But the logs were not out.
They were painted white, kind of like they do some places.
They make-- some people make blue.
Some people make pink.
Ours was kind of like the white.
But it was like cabin-like, because we had right beside it, we have a stable.
We had always three cows, because for milk and butter to have.
And-- and not everybody had-- well, the most everybody.
We were just kind of two families, which the rest of the village took care.
Well, this is kind of like one.
In those small villages, it's like this.
It's one bedroom-- one bathroom, a big one, which there was the oven to bake bread, like the-- like the Indian style with the thing and the-- and to cook, the stove built in.
And there were beds.
And there was a big table.
It was big.
And then was another room.
Now, they were sleeping like two together, sometimes three.
And no if company came, they put them-- well, in Yiddish you say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
You know?
Right.
So that's how it was.
In the summertime, there was in the stable fresh hay, or even outside.
Then-- and-- it was really-- we had a creek right in the front of the house.
We had a lot of pear trees and apple trees and around and walnuts they up.
There was a little hill up.
And we had a lot of field like right after the house and then separately, which was for hay to cut for the winter for the-- for the animals.
And so it was we had a creek, as I said, right in the front of the house.
In the summertime, we used to see about like when we didn't have any chores to do, we're sitting there and thinking.
Kept our feet in the water to cool ourselves off.
And we, the girls, we were doing embroideries, knitting, crocheting, whatever it had to be done.
And it was [INAUDIBLE].
Sometimes there was a little bridge.
And the-- the water was a little bit deeper than over the knees.
And we just in the afternoon just jumped in and-- well, couldn't swim but just dunk in in the cool water.
Yes.
Oh, yes, very, very happy.
Well, the religious practice, well, there were boys.
And the boys got a [INAUDIBLE].
That we called the [INAUDIBLE], the teacher.
He was the teacher.
And it was like from after Pesach till before the Rosh Hashanah.
And then after the Sukkot, there was high-- or the same or-- you know, or somebody else.
They went to the city.
And there was the-- the looking over the things and then through the winter.
And it was like this, that whoever had a boy, a child, everybody-- they divided the weeks, how many weeks and how many children.
So in every house, the teacher was in that family house, room and board.
If there were two-- two children, they had to-- to do a double.
They figured it out between themselves.
And the most-- the boys were going.
The girls were not-- they were-- the girls was like this.
We get picked up, whatever it is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My girlfriend went to a special-- not special.
I mean, she had [? their ?] two brothers were kind of in the yeshiva.
When they were home, they were teaching her [INAUDIBLE].
Because they were kind of younger, she-- they were still at home.
Well, when I was home, my brothers were not already.
They were-- well, one of them, which lived in Prague.
And the other one was working away.
So I surprised my parents with a Hebrew reading.
Well, we had geese and things.
So I used to go and mind the geese way out from the village.
Because as I say, it's just kind of country life.
So I use to switch in the [INAUDIBLE], and my, whatever I took lunch.
And I learned to read.
And I surprised my parents one day when I knew how to read.
And they were very happy.
And that's how it was.
Was there a synagogue or a beth midrash in the--
It was just a beth midrash.
It was kind of just one of the people had.
And they just didn't have any children.
They had one son, kind of, grown up-- in my time, what I remember.
I don't remember something way before.
And he donated like half of the house.
He left for himself two bedrooms and the two of them.
So one was for the women fixed and the other one was for the men.
They closed up-- I mean, opened the wall.
And they made the small, little window kind of just the women should know where the davening is-- what would you call it?
Don't know how to say it in English-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] the minyan, the davening.
And [INAUDIBLE] was just two of them.
It was my girlfriend's father and [INAUDIBLE] and my father.
Them two just could read the Torah.
So it was an Orthodox home?
Yeah, real Orthodox.
Well, [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE] where we went to-- to buy the other necessary things for the house, and like your clothing could be too, and the smaller thing.
But for the real one is [?
Mukacheve. ?]
Approximately, I-- I forgot about how much the kilometers.
It took like that.
We had to go to the train like an hour and a half and then to [INAUDIBLE], and that just by walking.
And from [INAUDIBLE] took an hour for the train.
In between was little villages just kind of thing, though.
But it took an hour.
Yeah.
Yes, I do.
Well, I had girlfriends, a lot of them.
And as I said, they were very nice.
And I specially when was kind of with them.
Not just me, there were-- a lot of the youngsters were kind of-- they-- we were like-- but there was.
There was something.
Like for instance, when the children were small.
When they were bigger, that's OK.
But you know when children are small, I don't know, in Yiddish, we have a-- a have a saying.
I don't know if I should say it.
They are saying, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
You know what it mean?
What they hear in the house, then they blurt out when the time comes.
So as I was playing, you know, when we were real little with the Gentile children, if they got angry, they started calling me names.
And they said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
And that means in Russian, Jews to Palestine.
Oh, then in a minute, we were you best friend again.
But it was there.
But the-- and the-- the bigger people, I mean the grownups, were very nice.
Yeah.
We had a lot of Gentile friends in the village.
Our neighbors were really good.
And we had a sheriff in the-- in the village.
That was the highest of like from the government and no police.
From the-- the police were from the second village thing.
So we were really very good.
Yes.
Yes.
Ivan Semik.
Well, when the time came like that he knew the situation from-- from the higher quarters that we're going to be taken away, he suggested once that he could hide three, four people.
So it was [INAUDIBLE] between ourselves kind of.
He knew approximately that which was, you know, like I had a very girlfriend.
The rest of the boys were away on that forced labor.
You know, like--
Yeah, things.
And so he said-- so we [INAUDIBLE].
He told that for my father.
And then we talked it over in the house.
And my parents said like this.
We are old.
And we'll go together.
We stay together.
Maybe you're gonna be a help for them.
Because the rest of them were all out of the house.
I was the only one when the-- in that time.
So that was that.
Yeah, and then when the-- then we left already from the house, everything was boarded up.
Inside the house, was-- I myself took the cows like a few days before.
There was a certain place.
And all the Jewish people had to bring their-- whoever had cows.
The-- the chickens, and the geese, and the ducks, that was left to whatever.
They were roaming around in the-- in the yard or in the backyard or wherever.
But those, they took away.
And I remember that.
I myself went there and helped my father, well, mother.
Well, I was always helping out from my father because that's what-- mother was in the house.

And so when they started to open the house and to confiscate whatever was useful for them from the house, the furniture and clothing, whatever, valuables, whatever, well, we tried to hide-- to give some for the other neighborhoods to hide.
But it was very strict, because every-- every day, we didn't have like here at the radio and they announce on the radio the news, wherever it's going on in-- in the city of wherever.
We had a town crier.
In the good times, too, if something came up, news for the-- you know, that the village has to be repaired, something.
In the good times, there was a town crier.
That's what they call it, with a--
Yeah.
And he was in a-- in a-- what is it called?
Like the musicians have it, too.
Gee.
And so they were kind of warning the Gentile people that whoever is gonna take something to hide for the Jewish people, they are gonna be punished.
So we hid.
So they took out from the house everything.
Well, that was in '30-- in '39 when the Hungarians came in.
No, because they-- let me.
I have to think about it.
Forgotten all about it, how that was going.
I remember when they came in.
And they said that the Hungarians gonna come in, that won't be good for the Jews.
And--
They didn't do right away things.

It just happened when the Germans really kind of-- well, they-- I-- I really don't know the dates and the politics, how it was then when the Germans took over the real Hungarians, and-- or it was-- I know in '39, the Hungarians came in.
When the Germans took over the Hungarians, like made that [INAUDIBLE] with them, I really don't know.
Well, it was the same thing for a while.
Because that was in '39.
And in '44, they took us away.
In-- in between, it was OK.
They were really-- we knew that they are big anti-Semites.
But they didn't bother.
As a matter of fact, like the officials, they were very good with the Jewish people.
Because the-- it's socialization kind of like.
They didn't have, you know, like they came in there.
So with the Jewish people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no.
Well, I put it that way, more than the peasants.
Kind of-- it was kind of like appropriate with them-- to-- to socialize.
No.
No.
No.
That started the winter-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's see, make the date '42 they took us away.
That was right in the beginning of '42.
Because that time when we started to wear-- I think that's when we start to wear the-- the-- the yellow star.
I remember having it on, but not exactly the day to-- when we put it on.
Because I know that-- that-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No.
It must have been in '41.
Because it's kind of too close from the taking away.
Because I know there was the Hungarians going to the front of Russia.
And they stopped in our village.
And we had a, I don't know, a high rank official in our house.
We gave one of those bedrooms to give them, and the horses there, and the stable.
And-- and my sister across the street, she lived.
And in their house was, you know, [? all ?] [? the way. ?] They took in the Jewish places, because the Jewish people had the houses more to accommodate them and-- and the food.
Well, they had their own kitchen.
But they liked the home cooked meals.
And they were very nice.

But I know that we had already that time the-- the yellow star, because I had a sister living a little bit further [INAUDIBLE], because the gentleman what he gave the house, he moved to the city, to [?
Mukacheve. ?] And my sister was living there by [INAUDIBLE].
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember like a Thursday night.
My sister didn't [INAUDIBLE] to bake bread.
And Thursday night, we had to prepare the bread for Friday morning to bake the challahs and the bread.
So it was not allowed for me to go to my sister to get what she wants to ask if [INAUDIBLE], and especially after 6 o'clock, or whatever it was, the curfew, was not allowed.
So the guy which was there, he was the-- he was cleaning the [INAUDIBLE] for that big captain, whatever.
He stayed in our house.
So he went with me.
Because if he went with me, that means that I was allowed to go there.
And that's where it start.
It was kind of like about--
And-- and they left.
Like, they were kind of like a week or two they were resting there.
And then they went towards the Polish [INAUDIBLE] border.
And-- and he knew, too.
He was throwing some words that big captain, or whatever it was, and not to worry.
And he was saying goodbye.
And he told for my parents there.
I was in that room, in that second room.
And when I heard what he was saying, I remember myself crying, stand there by the window.
I have that little piece where I doodle sometimes right by that window.
And I was crying, because it's nearing something.
And so after that right away, it kind-- it was almost like that Pesach time coming.
And we couldn't go to [INAUDIBLE] to that-- you know, like to buy things.
And if you went, you did not go on the sidewalk.
Yeah.
And we had the star.
Yeah.
Yes.
No.
No exceptions.
Well, he retired from that place already, from that what he was doing, the foreman and managing that stuff.
No, he did not work already at that time.
So-- because he retired and was just kind of like home.
He got a little pension from there.
And the rest of it, we-- we mined the fields.
No, I don't remember that.
I don't really remember.
I should have, because I was at my girlfriend like [INAUDIBLE], and I don't know who.
From that military?
Yes, Hungarian.
Yeah.
